detailedExemplarData.title: âTo what extent does the River Tillingbourne conform to the Bradshaw model?â

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is succinct, fully geographical and explicitly linked to the Bradshaw model, enabling clear hypothesis formation.
Introduction gives precise geographic context (River Tillingbourne length, drainage area) and justifies feasibility with accessibility, safety and time considerations.
Site map is clearly labelled with drainage-basin outline, legend and scale, and individual sampling points are identified, following essential cartographic conventions.
weaknesses: Map omits a north arrow; adding orientation would improve spatial clarity.
The aim statement could more explicitly mention the key Bradshaw variables to sharpen focus and alignment with the hypothesis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands (question formulation, geographic context, mapping) meet the top-band descriptors. Minor omissions (north arrow, aim detail) do not detract from the overall clarity and theoretical linkage, warranting a full mark of 3.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are clearly described and explicitly linked to the research question and Bradshaw model.
Standard field techniques were correctly applied, yielding usable primary data from multiple sites.
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks detail on spatial replication and selection criteria, limiting methodological transparency.
Equipment calibration and ethical permissions (landowner consent, safety protocols) are not fully documented.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methods are competently described with clear relevance to the question, and data collection techniques are correctly used but with limited replication and documentation. This aligns with band 2 for both strands, meriting a score of 2.

strengths: Data table covers all key Bradshaw variables, ensuring relevance for analysis.
A variety of appropriate presentation techniques (scatter graphs with trend lines, proportional circles, bar charts, inverted long-profile) are used effectively.
Most graphs include axes, units and titles, demonstrating general adherence to conventions.
weaknesses: Several graphs lack axis units and a proportional circle map misses a legend for size-scale interpretation.
OCR artifacts disrupt formula clarity; some table headers omit units.
Secondary data sources lack full citation and reliability assessment.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are largely relevant and presentations are appropriate, placing the work in the 3â4 band. Occasional errors in conventions and minor gaps prevent entry to the highest band, justifying a mark of 4 out of 6.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive statistics and Spearmanâs Rank correlations are applied appropriately, demonstrating sound statistical use.
Trends and anomalies are identified and explicitly linked to both the research question and Bradshaw theory.
Excellent theoretical integration in gradient and hydraulic radius analyses, with strong interpretation of SRCC values.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks confidence limits and error bars, limiting statistical completeness.
Reliance on secondary data and limited sample size introduce evidential gaps.
Discussion of anomalies (e.g., site 5) could be deepened with further morphological or error-analysis investigation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits robust descriptive and inferential techniques and strong data interpretation linked to theory, meeting the 5â6 band. Minor gaps in statistical rigor and completeness place it at the lower end of the band, earning 5 out of 8.

strengths: Conclusion clearly states that the river conforms to the Bradshaw model and cites percentage changes and statistical findings.
Succinct restatement of hypothesis and direct linkage to analytical results provides strong support.
weaknesses: Does not temper findings with fieldwork limitations (seasonality, single-day data), which would provide balanced perspective.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear and fully supported by the analysis, fulfilling the top-band descriptor. A minor contextual caveat does not undermine full marks, so a score of 2 is appropriate.

strengths: Evaluation proposes relevant improvements (e.g., digital flow meter, multiple gradient readings) linked to error reduction.
Strengths and weaknesses of key methods are outlined, demonstrating thoughtful methodological reflection.
weaknesses: Discussion covers only a subset of methods and lacks depth across the entire investigation.
Impacts of suggested improvements on overall study validity and the use of secondary data are not fully explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strengths and weaknesses are appropriately outlined and relevant improvements suggested, corresponding to band 2. Limited depth prevents reaching band 3, so a score of 2 is justified.

strandSelections.reasoning: Figure 2.1 provides a clearly labelled site map with drainage-basin outline, legend and scale bar; individual sites are numbered and named.  Although cartographic styling is basic, essential conventionsâorientation, key and scaleâare present, so the strand meets the highest descriptor for clear, convention-following locational mapping.

The student poses a clearly focussed, fully geographical question that is explicitly grounded in recognised fluvial theory (the Bradshaw Model).  A precise hypothesis is stated and all variables are defined, allowing measurable testing.  This satisfies the descriptor of a question âwith clear theoretical links allowing for hypothesis formation.â

A range of appropriate techniques is used: scatter graphs with trend lines, proportional circles, located bar charts and an inverted long-profile line.  These choices are suitable and generally effective, demonstrating an ability to select good forms for different data types, hence the upper end of band 3â4.

Standard riverâstudy techniques (tape, chain, clinometer, hydro-prop, float) were employed correctly and yielded usable data from ten sites.  Nonetheless, only six points were actually visited; four relied on secondary values, and no replication is mentioned, limiting reliability.  This corresponds to âtechniques correctly used but with minimal data collection.â

Descriptive statistics (means, percentages) and one inferential test (Spearmanâs Rank with significance level) are employed appropriately, showing sound though limited statistical proficiency.  Confidence limits or error bars are absent, preventing entry to the higher 5â6 band; the work therefore sits at the top of 3â4.

For each variable the student describes trends, identifies anomalies and links causes to Bradshaw theory (tributaries, erosion, roughness).  These explanations extend beyond simple description and explicitly relate patterns to both the research question and theory, satisfying the 5â6 descriptor; minor omissions keep the mark to 5 rather than 6.

The introduction specifies the River Tillingbourne, quotes its length (19 km) and drainage-basin area, and justifies feasibility in terms of accessibility, safety and time.  Ten individual sampling sites are later identified, demonstrating that primary data collection at specific points is realistic.  This level of locational precision fulfils the top-band requirement.

Strengths and weaknesses of two key methods are described with some explanation of how errors might have influenced reliability, representing an âappropriate outline.â  Discussion is limited to a couple of variables and lacks depth across the whole investigation, so the strand fits band 2.

The table of results covers all key Bradshaw variables and therefore most data are relevant to the research aim.  Yet the small spatial sample and mixture of primary and secondary sources leave some gaps, so the evidence is adequate rather than fully sufficient, matching the lower end of the 3â4 band.

The conclusion clearly states that the river conforms to the Bradshaw Model and explicitly references percentage changes and statistical results from the analysis section, fulfilling the requirement for a âclear conclusion fully supported by analysis.â

All five Bradshaw variables are analysed, yet sample size limitations and dependence on secondary figures create evidential gaps acknowledged by the student.  Hence the analysis is solid but not fully comprehensive, aligning with âanalysis with some gaps in evidenceâ at the upper end of band 3â4.

A methods table outlines equipment and step-by-step procedures for each variable and links these back to the Bradshaw Model, demonstrating clear relevance.  However, explanations of sampling strategy, temporal replication and theoretical justification are only brief, so the description is competent rather than comprehensive, placing it in band 2.

Axes, units and titles are mostly present, but some graphs lack axis units, one image is rotated, and percentage signs are inconsistent.  Such lapses indicate only occasional adherence to conventions, fitting the 1â2 band; the stronger of the two marks is awarded because most mistakes are minor rather than fundamental.

Concrete improvementsâmultiple gradient readings, use of a digital flow meterâare proposed and linked to reducing human error, meeting the descriptor for ârelevant improvement suggestions.â  However, impacts on overall study validity are only briefly noted, so the work does not reach the comprehensive level of band 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is succinct, geographical and explicitly linked to the Bradshaw model, providing a clear basis for hypothesis formation and measurable variables.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The aim statement is clear but could explicitly mention key Bradshaw variables to enhance focus and align more closely with the hypothesis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The contextual justification effectively integrates location specifics (drainage area, geology, land use, climate) with the Bradshaw model, demonstrating strong geographic grounding.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Linking the study to the IB syllabus option and drainage basin hydrology shows awareness of curriculum context and strengthens academic relevance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The methodology notes date and site visits but omits sampling strategy details (e.g., spatial replication, selection criteria). Expand to justify site selection rigorously.
The ethical considerations are broadly stated but lack reference to specific permissions (e.g., landowner consent) and health-and-safety protocols. Include these to strengthen methodological rigour.
Formula descriptions are interrupted by OCR artifacts (e.g., â4042.â). Present mathematical expressions clearly using LaTeX (e.g., $$\text{CSA} = \text{width} \times \text{depth}$$).
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The investigative rationale (ârelationship between discharge and distance downstreamâ) is concise and guides the analysis effectively.
Figure 14âs scatter graph lacks axis units for both distance (km) and discharge (mÂ³/s). Add units on both axes to improve interpretability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The site map uses legend and scale but omits a north arrow, which is an essential cartographic convention. Add orientation to improve spatial clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The equipment list is comprehensive but does not detail instrument accuracy or calibration procedures (e.g., clinometer precision). Include specifications to ensure data reliability.
The figure for secondary data lacks citation of data sources and publication dates. Provide full references and assess the reliability of these external data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The results table is detailed but several column headers (e.g., CSA, perimeter) are missing units. Ensure every variable heading specifies its unit for clarity and convention adherence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The evaluation of data presentation notes strengths of scatter and proportional circle graphs but could assess the limitations of using secondary data in analysis.
The Spearmanâs Rank caption notes significance but omits test statistics and critical values. Include a table of SRCC calculations to demonstrate understanding and transparency.
The analysis links the high SRCC value (0.988 at 1% significance) directly to hypothesis testing, demonstrating strong statistical interpretation.
At site 5, attributing the anomaly solely to proximity is unsupported. Investigate local channel morphology or human abstraction that may explain reduced discharge.
The CSA scatter graph paired with SRCC (0.939 at 1% level) evidences suitable use of descriptive and inferential techniques.
The anomaly at site 5 is attributed to a meander without considering measurement error or planform variation; consider repeating crossâsections and mapping channel shape.
The hydraulic radius bar chart conveys percentage change but a scatter plot would better illustrate continuous downstream variation and support statistical analysis.
The hydraulic radius analysis effectively links channel efficiency and roughness reduction to theoretical expectations, showing sound data interpretation.
The proportional circle map for velocity is a strong spatial representation, facilitating rapid visual comparison of flow speeds across sites.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The proportional circle map lacks a legend indicating circle sizes relative to velocity values; add a scale to clarify quantitative comparisons.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The inverted line graph for gradient may confuse readers; consider a standard orientation with gradient on the y-axis decreasing downstream to align with conventional presentation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The gradient analysis correctly identifies the negative downstream trend and links it to energy gradients per the Bradshaw modelâexcellent theoretical integration.
The evaluation thoroughly discusses gradient and velocity method weaknesses with relevant improvement strategies, demonstrating sound methodological reflection.
The one-day fieldwork limitation is noted but should be reflected in the conclusion to temper the findings and acknowledge seasonal variability implications.
The conclusion succinctly restates that the River Tillingbourne conforms to the Bradshaw model, citing percentage changes and linking back to the hypothesisâwell supported.
